engine.maia: Maia-1 rating-band networks 1100-1900 via zerofish WASM lc0, nodes=1

accuracy.method: mean of volatility-weighted mean and harmonic mean of per-move accuracies (Lichess formulas)

mistakes.title: Stepped next to the enemy bishop instead of taking it
mistakes.explanation: You are winning easily here — an extra bishop, a passed pawn, an active king. Black's bishop sits undefended on b8, and 66.Bxb8 simply removes it: your king mops up the pawns and the game is over. Instead 66.Be5?? parks your bishop beside Black's, and 66…Bxe5 takes it off. After 67.dxe5 you are down to a bare king-and-pawn race that Black wins — the evaluation flips from +7.6 to hopeless in one move. When the opponent has a loose piece, the move is almost always to capture it, not to manoeuvre around it.

takeaways.lesson: If an enemy piece is hanging, take it — don't get fancy.
takeaways.detail: Before every move in a winning position, scan for undefended enemy pieces. Here Black's b8-bishop was free; 66.Bxb8 ends the game. Manoeuvres can wait — material in hand wins endings.

takeaways.lesson: Before you move a piece, check what it will be standing next to.
takeaways.detail: 66.Be5 put your bishop on a square attacked by Black's bishop, for nothing in return. Make the last question before you release a piece: 'On its new square, can anything capture it?'

mistakes.title: Shuffled a pawn while your king sat idle
mistakes.explanation: With a passed pawn on b7 and an extra bishop, this ending is winning — but only if the king joins the fight. 63.Kf3! marches the king toward Black's pawns and the win is straightforward. 63.g3?? is a do-nothing pawn move: it improves nothing, gives Black's king a free tempo to get active, and the engine's assessment crashes from +7 to dead equal. In king-and-pawn and minor-piece endings the king is a fighting piece — leaving it at home is how won positions slip to draws.

takeaways.lesson: In the endgame, activate your king before you touch a pawn.
takeaways.detail: Once queens are off, march the king toward the action. Ask each move: 'Is my king doing something?' If not, a king move is usually better than a pawn shuffle.

takeaways.lesson: Don't make waiting moves when you have a concrete plan.
takeaways.detail: You had a clear target — Black's pawns and the queening of b7. Aimless moves like 63.g3 hand the initiative back. Identify the plan, then play the move that advances it.

mistakes.title: Offered a queen trade when you could just win the queen
mistakes.explanation: Black's queen has wandered to e5 — straight into the line of your d4-pawn. 52.dxe5 captures it for free and leaves you mating in a handful of moves (the engine reads a forced mate here). Instead 52.Qe3?? offered an even queen trade; after 52…Qxe3+ 53.Bxe3 you are still winning, but you have swapped a forced mate and a free queen for a long technical grind — the same grind you went on to lose. When you have a crushing attack, don't help the opponent simplify: keep the pieces on and finish.

takeaways.lesson: Look for captures before anything else — the free queen is often right there.
takeaways.detail: Every move, list the opponent's undefended pieces and every capture you have. 52.dxe5 wins the queen outright; a 10-second capture-check finds it.

takeaways.lesson: When you're attacking, keep the queens on the board.
takeaways.detail: Trading queens is what the defender wants. If you have a mating attack or a big material edge, avoid trades that ease the pressure — the extra pieces are your winning tools, not a liability.

highlights.note: After Stockfish's odd 2…Qe7 you took the whole centre with 3.e4 — the engine's first choice, and the reason you stood clearly better out of the opening.
In the bishop endgame you marched your king up the board instead of pushing pawns — exactly right: in the endgame the king is a fighting piece.

openingReport.note: Against 2…Qe7 — out of book on move 2 — your 3.e4 was the perfect answer (see below). The first improvable decision was 5.Ng5: the same knight's second move before any other piece developed, when 5.Bg5 hit the queen and developed something new. You have now faced this …e6 French-style setup in two analyzed games.
